behaviors.description: Log entries with the same trace ID are grouped together regardless of which microservice emitted them.
behaviors.justification: This tests pure correlation logic—matching and grouping by trace ID. Real failures (case sensitivity, type mismatch, incorrect grouping) are in the algorithm, not in log ingestion mechanics. Can be tested by constructing log objects from different service sources and verifying they correlate correctly, with no actual microservices or I/O required.
behaviors.test_description: Create a set of log entries with identical trace IDs from different simulated services (e.g., ServiceA, ServiceB, ServiceC), ensure they are grouped together correctly, and verify that entries with different trace IDs are not mixed in.

behaviors.description: The timeline view orders entries by timestamp, correctly handling clock skew of up to 500ms between services.
behaviors.justification: This is a sorting and timestamp-comparison algorithm. Real failures (wrong sort order, broken skew tolerance, off-by-one) emerge from the logic, not from real clock sources. Clock skew can be simulated deterministically with constructed timestamps (e.g., T and T+250ms from different sources). No real system time or synchronization service needed.
behaviors.test_description: Create log entries with timestamps that simulate clock skew across services (e.g., same event logged at T+0 on Service A and T+250ms on Service B), verify the timeline orders them correctly, and confirm that the skew tolerance of 500ms correctly handles both within-tolerance and out-of-tolerance cases.

behaviors.description: A latency spike (p99 above 3x the baseline) is detected and flagged in the anomaly report.
behaviors.justification: This is statistical computation on in-memory data. Real failures (wrong percentile calculation, incorrect threshold comparison, baseline miscalculation) are purely algorithmic. No real services, no I/O, and no dependency on system behavior—just test data with known latency values.
behaviors.test_description: Create log entries with a known baseline latency (e.g., 100ms) and a spike (e.g., 350ms, which is 3.5x baseline). Verify that the p99 latency is calculated correctly and that the anomaly detection correctly flags the spike. Test edge cases: baseline all equal values, single spike among baseline entries, and borderline cases (exactly 3.0x and 2.99x).

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON log entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline, and the count of skipped entries is reported.
behaviors.justification: This is error-handling business logic, not I/O behavior. Real failures are: exception not caught (crash), skip count not incremented, or skipped entries silently lost. All are caught by passing malformed JSON strings directly to the parser—no file or network I/O needed.
behaviors.test_description: Feed the ingestion pipeline a mix of valid and malformed JSON entries (e.g., truncated JSON, missing fields, invalid syntax). Verify the pipeline processes all valid entries correctly, does not crash on malformed input, and the reported skip count matches the number of malformed entries.

behaviors.description: The tool handles a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind.
behaviors.justification: Real failure modes—buffer overflow (entries dropped), blocking I/O (pipeline stalls), unbounded queue (memory leak), file descriptor exhaustion—require actual I/O and queue mechanics to surface. A Unit test with 10K in-memory objects completes instantly and masks real bottlenecks. Integration level (with real queue, file I/O, in-process) is needed to catch when the system can't keep pace under sustained load.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a real input queue and file source, feed entries at 10,000/sec sustained rate for a measurable duration (e.g., 10 seconds = 100,000 entries). Measure end-to-end processing latency and verify: (1) no entries are dropped from the queue, (2) the system processes entries as fast as they arrive without falling behind, (3) queue size remains bounded, and (4) memory usage stays within acceptable limits throughout.
